---
title: "Lab 3 Leaderboard — AY26 Q3"
subtitle: "USV Waypoint Mission Challenge — Day 1 (2026-06-03)"
---

[← Back to Lab 3 overview](index.qmd)

## Current standings

Best course time per team on challenge day 1 (2026-06-03). Times are
the autopilot's own WP1-to-WP7 elapsed, as logged in the `MSG`
"Reached waypoint #N" events — see [Scoring notes](#scoring-notes)
below for why this is the right source of truth.

| Rank | Team | Best time | UTC time of run | Log |
|:---:|---|---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | cyborgs | 01:15 (75.2 s) | 2026-06-03 18:41:27 | `00000051.BIN` |
| 2 | team3 | 01:43 (103.4 s) | 2026-06-03 18:26:57 | `00000039.BIN` |
| 3 | blackpearl | 03:26 (205.8 s) | 2026-06-03 18:28:14 | `00000049.BIN` |
|  | *Instructor benchmark* | *03:13 (193.4 s)* | *2026-05-29 (prototype day)* | — |

Headline: every team beat the instructor benchmark. cyborgs more than
halved it. team3's best run was ~90 seconds faster than the instructor
time as well.

## Per-team breakdown

Parameter-change summaries describe what each team tuned relative to
the lab baseline `.param`, keeping the specific parameter names and
values out of view. The point is the *direction* of each team's
tuning, not a recipe.

### cyborgs

Seven complete-course runs on challenge day, with a clear improvement
arc as they tuned.

| # | Time | UTC | Log |
|:-:|---:|:--|:--|
| 1 | 03:15 (195.2 s) | 2026-06-03 18:31:10 | `00000048.BIN` |
| 2 | 01:28 (87.8 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:37:24 | `00000049.BIN` |
| 3 | 01:15 (75.2 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:41:27 | `00000051.BIN` |
| 4 | 01:19 (79.2 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:43:57 | `00000052.BIN` |
| 5 | 01:15 (75.2 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:49:04 | `00000053.BIN` |
| 6 | 01:22 (81.6 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:54:05 | `00000057.BIN` |
| 7 | 01:20 (79.5 s)  | 2026-06-03 18:58:56 | `00000058.BIN` |

Tuning direction relative to baseline:

- Applied the standard fix to the stick-to-rate scaling.
- Disabled the speed-axis rate limiter.
- Raised the waypoint cruise speed modestly (a small multiple of baseline).
- Zeroed out the speed-loop feedforward gain (relying entirely on PID closure).
- Raised the pivot-turn threshold so moderate course-changes are handled with continuous steering instead of stop-and-pivot.

The arc — first run baseline at ~195 s, then a step-change to ~88 s once they committed to their tuning, then six runs clustered tightly in the 75-82 s range — looks like a team that iterated to a good configuration and then exercised it for consistency.

### team3

Four complete-course runs on challenge day, all clustered tightly in the 103-108 s range.

| # | Time | UTC | Log |
|:-:|---:|:--|:--|
| 1 | 01:48 (108.0 s) | 2026-06-03 18:20:43 | `00000037.BIN` |
| 2 | 01:43 (103.4 s) | 2026-06-03 18:26:57 | `00000039.BIN` |
| 3 | 01:45 (104.5 s) | 2026-06-03 18:34:12 | `00000041.BIN` |
| 4 | 01:44 (104.3 s) | 2026-06-03 18:42:15 | `00000043.BIN` |

Tuning direction relative to baseline:

- Applied the standard stick-to-rate fix.
- Disabled the speed-axis rate limiter.
- Pushed both the cruise-speed scaling and the waypoint cruise speed to very high values — well above the boat's nominal cruise.
- Maxed out the throttle baseline bias.
- Disabled the pivot-turn feature entirely.
- Slashed the steering proportional and integral gains, sharply raised the steering feedforward, and added a derivative term.
- Switched the primary heading reference between compass instances.

team3 chose to commit to high-speed transit and accept that the boat would overshoot every waypoint. The autopilot was happy with that — it advanced through the mission using perpendicular-crossing detection rather than waiting for the boat to dip inside the strict acceptance radius. The on-water observation that "team3 looked fast" was correct.

It is worth singling out the **combination** that team3 produced: by far the most aggressive tuning of any team — speeds set well above the boat's nominal cruise, throttle baseline maxed, steering loop substantially restructured — *and* the most consistent lap-to-lap results on the leaderboard. Their four complete runs span 4.6 seconds end-to-end (103.4 s to 108.0 s), a tighter spread than any other team's runs. Aggressive and unreliable is the usual pairing in autonomous-vehicle tuning; aggressive and *repeatable* is harder, and team3 demonstrated it.

That they didn't win on raw time is a separate observation. Whatever cyborgs was doing at the steering layer let them carry similar (or higher) average speed through the course geometry with cleaner traces through the corners — but neither configuration "broke" the autopilot, and both finished well under the instructor benchmark.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Note on the original analysis.** Day-1's first leaderboard initially showed team3 as "DNF". That was a bug in our scoring script — it required each waypoint to be physically entered within the 1 m acceptance radius, but team3's boat was traveling fast enough that it overshot most waypoints without entering the strict radius. The autopilot itself, which uses both radius entry and perpendicular crossing, advanced through all 7 waypoints just fine; we just weren't reading its `MSG` log. The script has since been corrected; see [Scoring notes](#scoring-notes).
:::

### blackpearl

One complete-course run on challenge day.

| # | Time | UTC | Log |
|:-:|---:|:--|:--|
| 1 | 03:26 (205.8 s) | 2026-06-03 18:28:14 | `00000049.BIN` |

Tuning direction relative to baseline:

- Applied the standard stick-to-rate fix.
- Disabled the speed-axis rate limiter.
- Roughly doubled the cruise-speed scaling and significantly raised the waypoint cruise speed.
- Added a constant throttle baseline bias on top of the per-step feedforward.
- Heavily retuned the speed-loop PID — substantially more aggressive across proportional, integral, derivative, and feedforward.
- Reduced the steering PID gains while raising the steering feedforward.
- Re-enabled the R/C-loss failsafe (the procedure recommends turning this off for the challenge course).

blackpearl's only complete run came in about 30 seconds slower than cyborgs' early baseline run and roughly 2-3x slower than what cyborgs and team3 settled into. The very aggressive throttle-stack tuning may have been spending control effort on the speed loop that wasn't translating into shorter course time, or producing overshoot that complicated the steering layer's job.

## What the leaderboard tells us

Day 1's spread was wide — 75 s to 206 s — and the order isn't a simple "more aggressive is faster" or "more conservative is faster." Two takeaways:

- The winning configuration (cyborgs) was *modest* on cruise speed but had a steering-loop tune that handled the corners well at the speeds they did use.
- team3's configuration was *very* aggressive on speed and accepted that the boat would overshoot waypoints — and it worked, because the autopilot's mission-advancement logic accepts perpendicular crossings, not just radius entry.

blackpearl's heavy speed-loop retune didn't pay off relative to the more measured approaches. Worth digging into the per-run logs to understand whether the limiting factor was speed-loop saturation, steering response, or trajectory shape at the corners.

## Scoring notes

A complete-course run is one where the autopilot's `MSG` log contains
`"Reached waypoint #N"` for N = 1 .. 7 in that order. This is the
autopilot's own mission-advancement signal, which uses *both* of:

- The boat entering the waypoint's acceptance radius (`WP_RADIUS`).
- The boat crossing the perpendicular at the waypoint (overshoot detection).

The second condition matters at speed: at this course's geometry, a
boat moving above ~3 m/s can legitimately overshoot a waypoint without
ever dipping inside a 1 m radius. The script used to ignore this case
and undercounted finishes. It has since been corrected to use the
autopilot's events directly; see
[`bin_coursetime.py`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/utils/ardu_utils/bin_coursetime.py).

## Notes

- The 192-193 s instructor benchmark is a prototype-day run (2026-05-29) using the baseline parameter file. It is the time-to-beat for the challenge, not part of the AY26 Q3 team standings. The 2026-05-29 result is preserved in the raw data but excluded from the per-team standings on this page.
- Per-team parameter summaries are drawn from a representative challenge-day log per team; the underlying logs are in `/data/ME2801_USV/2026_06_03_challengeday1/logs/` (instructor-only access).
- Day 2 results, when available, will be appended in a follow-on table above.
